chunk_summary: Psychosocial and feeding risks in pediatric food allergy and elimination diets require screening to prevent unsafe behaviors, ARFID, and excessive restriction. Interventions should prioritize safety, mental health, and quality of life improvements.
learning_objectives: Understand psychosocial impacts of food allergy and elimination diets in children and adolescents.
Identify signs of avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID) in long-term elimination diets.
Develop strategies for safe dietary management and psychosocial support.
Assess adolescent autonomy and risk-taking behaviors related to food allergy management.
key_facts: Psychosocial burden in pediatric food allergy can lead to unsafe behaviors or excessive restriction.
ARFID features include reduced food variety, prolonged meal times, fear of choking, weight loss, and distress around eating.
Escalation to broader elimination diets is contraindicated when ARFID features are present.
Adolescents require private assessment for autonomy, peer pressure, and intentional risk-taking behaviors.
Interventions include diet simplification, skill-based reintroduction planning, and coordinated care with dietitians and mental health professionals.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Psychosocial burden is common in pediatric food allergy and can become a driver of unsafe behavior (risk-taking) or excessive restriction. Screen at follow-up using a few consistent questions: Does the child or caregiver avoid social eating, school events, or travel due to fear of reactions? (Reference 1)
document_key_topics: Safe dietary management in elimination diets
Adolescent autonomy and risk-taking in food allergy
